Journal of Orthopaedic Research 
2~235-246, Raven Press, New York 0 1984 Orthopaedic Research Society 
Characterization of Growth Plate Mitochondria 
*J L Stambough, *C T Brighton, *J P Iannotti, and tB T Storey 
Departments of *Orthopaedic Surgery and PObstetrical and Gynecological Research, University of Pennsylvania 
School of Medicine, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 
Summary: The growth plate chondrocyte plays a central role in growth plate 
function The purpose of this study was to characterize the respiratory and 
calcium transport properties of isolated mammalian growth plate chondrocytes 
and mitochondria obtained from these cells and to quantitate the mitochondrial 
weight and volume fraction in each zone of the growth plate A new method 
was developed for isolation of mitochondria from chondrocyte suspensions 
Isolated chondrocyte mitochondria demonstrated an eightfold increase in ox- 
ygen consumption in response to calcium and a two- to threefold increase in 
oxygen consumption in response to adenosine diphosphate Similar responses 
were observed in chondrocytes treated with digitonin The mitochondrial pro- 
tein content of the growth plate and hyaline cartilage chondrocytes is signifi- 
cantly less than hepatocytes Conversely, the chondrocyte mitochondrial cy- 
tochrome aa3 content is similar to mitochondria from a wide variety of sources 
A zonal analysis of the growth plate demonstrates an increase in the mito- 
chondrial weight (protein) fraction from the reserve to the hypertrophic zone 
whereas the mitochondrial volume fraction decreases from the reserve to the 
hypertrophic zone The findings of this study emphasize the dependence of 
chondrocytes on glycolysis as a prime energy source and support the concept 
that chondrocyte mitochondria have become specialized in the process of ma- 
trix calcification Key Words: Growth plate chondrocyte-Mitochondria1 iso- 
lation-Chondrocyte metabolism-Cytochrome aa,-Oxygen consumption 
The chondrocyte has a central role in growth 
plate structure and function It has been shown to 
be the source of all matrix vesicles, proteins, and 
enzyme systems (4,45) The chondrocyte mitochon- 
dria have been postulated to play a crucial role in 
matrix calcification through matrix macromolecular 
biosynthesis and intracellular calcium transport 
(45) In spite of its important function in growth 
plate physiology, little quantitative data are avail- 
able regarding the bioenergetic characteristics of 
mammalian chondrocyte Several qualitative and 
semiquantitative studies have defined some of the 
biochemical characteristics of the growth plate 
Address correspondence and reprint requests to Dr Stam- 
bough at Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of 
Pennsylvania School of Medicine, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 
19104 chondrocyte and its subcellular organelles 
(1,5,7,15,21,23,36,44) The basic oxygen utilization 
patterns and adenosine diphosphate (ADP) or cal- 
cium induced respiratory ratios have not been de- 
fined The last reported respiratory studies in mam- 
malian cartilage were published a half century ago 
based on analysis of cartilage slices (10,34) 
Chondrocytes contain mitochondria and so re- 
spire, but the few studies that have addressed the 
question of chondrocyte bioenergetics indicate that 
glycolysis rather than oxidative phosphorylation is 
the main source of intracellular adenosine triphos- 
phate (ATP) (15,21,22,33) This raises the question of 
whether the mitochondria have become specialized 
in the mineralization process by means of their 
ability to accumulate Ca2+ Semiquantitative his- 
tochemical studies on the rat costochondral junc- 
235 236 .I L STAMBOUGH ET AL 
tion using pyroantimonate have given support to 
this possibility (5,6) The low mitochondrial content 
of the chondrocytes, as determined by the point- 
counting technique (8), is also consistent with such 
specialization But experimental support for the 
role of mitochondria in mineralization in the mam- 
malian growth plate requires isolation of these or- 
ganelles and of the chondrocytes so that mitochon- 
drial calcium ion (Ca2+) transport may be quanti- 
tated under controlled conditions 
It is the purpose of this study to present a method 
for the isolation of intact chondrocytes from hyaline 
and growth plate cartilage and to describe a method 
of isolating mitochondria from these cells In addi- 
tion, the respiratory activity of the mitochondrial 
preparations and the cells from which they are de- 
rived are characterized An accurate assessment of 
intracellular mitochondrial content is calculated 
from the measured cytochrome aa3 content of the 
isolated chondrocytes and their purified mitochon- 
dria 
MATERIALS 
Potassium chloride electrolyte buffer solution 
contained 130 mM KCl, 5 mM K,P04, 1 mM MgCl,, 
and 20 mM HEPES (N-2-hydroethylpiperazine-N- 
2-ethanesulfonic acid) The medium was buffered to 
a pH 7.6 and filtered through a Chelex-100 column 
three times to decrease the calcium contamination 
Magnesium chloride (1 mM) was added after Chelex 
filtration The calcium contamination was deter- 
mined to be 12 km by atomic absorption spectro- 
photometry All reagents were analytic grade and 
were purchased from Sigma Chemical Company 
Calcium magnesium free (CMF) Hanks and CMF- 
TCM (tissue culture medium-modified Eagles me- 
dium and Earles balanced salt solution (BSS) were 
purchased from Gibco and sterilized by millipore 
filtration CMF-TCM contained gentamicin (50 kgl 
ml)

**2. Extraction of Steps:**

2. Isolate chondrocyte mitochondria and observe an eightfold increase in oxygen consumption in response to calcium and a two- to threefold increase in oxygen consumption in response to adenosine diphosphate.

6. Perform a zonal analysis of the growth plate and observe an increase in the mitochondrial weight (protein) fraction from the reserve to the hypertrophic zone, while the mitochondrial volume fraction decreases from the reserve to the hypertrophic zone.

summary: Summary 3: The Journal of Orthopaedic Research published a study in 1984 by J.L. Stambough, C.T. Brighton, J.P. Iannotti, and T.B. Storey from the Departments of Orthopaedic Surgery and Obstetrical and Gynecological Research at the University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine in Philadelphia. The study focused on the characterization of growth plate chondrocytes and mitochondria. They isolated mitochondria from chondrocyte suspensions and found that they demonstrated an eightfold increase in oxygen consumption with calcium and a two- to threefold increase with adenosine diphosphate. Similar responses were observed in digitonin-treated chondrocytes. The mitochondrial protein content in growth plate chondrocytes was significantly less than hepatocytes, but the chondrocyte mitochondrial cytochrome aa3 content was similar to other sources. A zonal analysis of the growth plate revealed an increase in mitochondrial weight fraction from the reserve to the hypertrophic zone, while the mitochondrial volume fraction decreased. The study highlighted the dependence of chondrocytes on glycolysis as a prime energy source and the specialization of chondrocyte mitochondria in matrix calcification.

Chunk # 1.

METHODS 
Chondrocyte Isolation 
One- to three-week-old male Holstein calves 
were sacrificed with intravenous pentobarbital and 
the hyaline cartilage ribs and costochondral junc- 
tions were removed under strict sterile conditions 
Each cartilage tissue type was carefully freed of all 
perichondrial soft tissue and diced into 1-2 mm 
pieces Fifteen to twenty grams of cartilage was placed into a modified Green digestion chamber (19) 
containing 250 ml of digestion medium and inter- 
mittently agitated with a magnetic stir bar The ma- 
trix surrounding the chondrocytes was digested by 
sequential enzyme treatments in an incubator 
(Forma Scientific Model 3028) maintained at 37°C 
and 95% humidity and gassed with 5% CO, in air 
The enzymatic digestion for all cartilage tissue 
types consisted first of an incubation in CMF Hanks 
with 2.5 mM EGTA for 15 min The medium was 
removed and the cartilage was reincubated for an 
additional 15 min This was followed with an incu- 
bation in CMF-TCM containing 0.1% trypsin (Wor- 
thington) for 30 min and 300 U/ml 0.1% hyaluroni- 
dase (Worthington) for 60 min The final incubation 
was in CMF-TCM containing 0.5 mM CaC1, with 
0.2% crude clostridial collagenase (Worthington 
Type 11) for 18 h (The matrix digestion scheme uti- 
lizing trypsin, hyaluronidase, and collagenase was 
developed in the McKay Laboratory of Ortho- 
paedic Research based on pilot studies The addi- 
tion of trypsin was intended to initiate the matrix 
digestion followed by hyaluronidase Pilot studies 
demonstrated no change in trypan blue exclusion 
with exposure to trypsin or hyaluronidase in these 
quantities The length of digestion was directly re- 
lated to the quantity of cartilage digested and animal 
species.) 
The matrix-free cells were collected by centrifu- 
gation at 600 x g and washed twice at 4°C in CMF 
Hanks BSS or KC1 buffer, depending on their final 
use The cells were stored for a maximum of 4 h 
prior to use Plasma membrane intactness was mon- 
itored by trypan blue exclusion This method of cell 
isolation routinely yielded chondrocyte populations 
with more than 95% trypan blue exclusion 
Chondrocytes from the reserve, proliferative, and 
hypertrophic zones of the growth plate were ob- 
tained by first sectioning the zones from the growth 
plate by means of a precision controlled guillotine 
apparatus (38) The cells were isolated from each 
section by the method described above Represen- 
tative sections before digestion were examined his- 
tologically and found consistently to have more 
than 95% of the chondrocyte type characteristic of 
its zone , 
Isolated Hepatocytes 
Hepatocytes were obtained from male guinea 
pigs by a modification of the method of Berry and 
Friend (3) The liver was perfused in situ with CMF 
J Orthop Res, Voi 2, No 3, 1984 GROWTH PLATE MITOCHONDRIA 
Digitonin 
60 psi 237 
Hanks containing 0.5 mM EGTA for 15 min, fol- 
lowed by CMF Hanks containing 0.1% crude col- 
lagenase (Worthington Type 11) for 30 min, in a 
single pass through the hepatic portal vein The 
cells were gently disaggregated and isolated from 
the liver in CMF Hanks at 4°C The hepatocytes 
were washed twice and centrifugated at 600 x g 
Maximum storage time prior to use was 4 h 
Isolation of Chondrocyte Mitochondria 
Mitochondria were obtained from isolated chon- 
drocytes by pressure disruption of digitonin-treated 
cells, using a modified technique described by Zu- 
urendonk et al (46), which has been successfully 
applied to hepatocytes (41) (see Fig 1) Chondro- 
cytes washed in KC1 buffer were suspended at a 
concentration of 30-35 x lo6 cells/ml (4-5 mg cell 
protein/ml) in the KCl buffer containing 1 mM 
EGTA and 0.05% bovine serum albumin (BSA) 
Digitonin (Sigma) was added at a level of 5-7 pg/ 
lo6 cells to selectively render the plasma membrane 
permeable, and the cells were incubated at 4°C for 
30 min The membrane in plasma membrane per- 
meability was monitored by the uptake of trypan 
blue The cells were disrupted by extrusion of 5-8 
ml aliquots of the cell suspension at a constant pres- 
sure through a 23-gauge syringe needle, utilizing a 
Millipore Sweenex 50 pressure filtration system 
modified for small sample filtration The extent of 
cell membrane disruption was monitored by as- 
saying for lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) activity (2) 
in the supernatant from the 9,000 g centrifugation 
step (see below) Mitochondria1 disruption was 
monitored by assaying for citrate synthase activity 
(37) in the same supernatant; whereas mitochon- 
drial yield was monitored by assaying for this ac- 
tivity in the isolated mitochondria after sonication 
The effect of pressure on cell disruption is shown 
in Fig 2 The optimum balance between cell dis- 
ruption, yield of mitochondria, and disruption of 
mitochondria lay at 60-70 psi This pressure range 
was the one adopted for the standardized chondro- 
cyte disruption procedure

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Sacrifice one- to three-week-old male Holstein calves with intravenous pentobarbital.
2. Remove the hyaline cartilage ribs and costochondral junctions under sterile conditions.
3. Carefully remove all perichondrial soft tissue from each cartilage tissue type.
4. Dice the cartilage into 1-2 mm pieces.
5. Place 15-20 grams of cartilage into a modified Green digestion chamber containing 250 ml of digestion medium.
6. Intermittently agitate the cartilage with a magnetic stir bar.
7. Digest the matrix surrounding the chondrocytes by sequential enzyme treatments in an incubator maintained at 37°C and 95% humidity, gassed with 5% CO2 in air.
8. The enzymatic digestion for all cartilage tissue types consists of the following steps:
   a. Incubate in CMF Hanks with 2.5 mM EGTA for 15 min.
   b. Remove the medium and reincubate for an additional 15 min.
   c. Incubate in CMF-TCM containing 0.1% trypsin for 30 min.
   d. Incubate in CMF-TCM containing 300 U/ml 0.1% hyaluronidase for 60 min.
   e. Incubate in CMF-TCM containing 0.5 mM CaCl2 with 0.2% crude clostridial collagenase for 18 h.
9. Collect the matrix-free cells by centrifugation at 600 x g.
10. Wash the cells twice at 4°C in CMF Hanks BSS or KC1 buffer, depending on their final use.
11. Store the cells for a maximum of 4 h prior to use.
12. Monitor plasma membrane intactness by trypan blue exclusion, ensuring chondrocyte populations with more than 95% trypan blue exclusion.
13. Obtain chondrocytes from the reserve, proliferative, and hypertrophic zones of the growth plate by sectioning the zones from the growth plate using a precision-controlled guillotine apparatus.
14. Isolate the cells from each section using the method described above.
15. Examine representative sections before digestion histologically to confirm the presence of more than 95% of the chondrocyte type characteristic of its zone.
16. Obtain hepatocytes from male guinea pigs using the following steps:
   a. Perfuse the liver in situ with CMF Hanks containing 0.5 mM EGTA for 15 min.
   b. Perfuse the liver with CMF Hanks containing 0.1% crude collagenase for 30 min in a single pass through the hepatic portal vein.
   c. Gently disaggregate and isolate the cells from the liver in CMF Hanks at 4°C.
   d. Wash the hepatocytes twice and centrifuge at 600 x g.
   e. Store the hepatocytes for a maximum of 4 h prior to use.
17. Obtain mitochondria from isolated chondrocytes using the following steps:
   a. Wash chondrocytes in KC1 buffer and suspend them in KCl buffer containing 1 mM EGTA and 0.05% bovine serum albumin (BSA) at a concentration of 30-35 x 10^6 cells/ml (4-5 mg cell protein/ml).
   b. Add digitonin at a level of 5-7 μg/10^6 cells to selectively render the plasma membrane permeable.
   c. Incubate the cells at 4°C for 30 min.
   d. Monitor plasma membrane permeability by the uptake of trypan blue.
   e. Disrupt the cells by extrusion of 5-8 ml aliquots of the cell

summary:
Summary 4: Chondrocyte isolation involves sacrificing male Holstein calves and removing the hyaline cartilage ribs and costochondral junctions under strict sterile conditions. The perichondrial soft tissue is carefully removed, and the cartilage is diced into small pieces. The diced cartilage is then digested in a modified Green digestion chamber using a digestion medium. Enzyme treatments, including incubations in CMF Hanks with EGTA, CMF-TCM with trypsin and hyaluronidase, and CMF-TCM with CaCl2 and crude clostridial collagenase, are performed to digest the matrix surrounding the chondrocytes. The length of digestion is determined by the quantity of cartilage and animal species. The matrix-free cells are collected, washed, and stored for up to 4 hours. The viability of the cells is monitored using trypan blue exclusion. Chondrocyte populations with high trypan blue exclusion are consistently obtained.

Hepatocytes are obtained from male guinea pigs by perfusing the liver with CMF Hanks containing EGTA and crude collagenase. The isolated hepatocytes are gently disaggregated and washed before storage for up to 4 hours.

Chondrocyte mitochondria are obtained by pressure disruption of digitonin-treated cells. Chondrocytes washed in KC1 buffer are incubated with digitonin to permeabilize the plasma membrane. The cells are then disrupted by extrusion through a syringe needle. The extent of cell membrane disruption is monitored using lactate dehydrogenase activity. The optimal pressure for disruption is found to be 60-70 psi.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The weight fraction of 0.23 for hepatocytes agrees with that reported by Murphy et al, indicating the method's reliability.
2. The volume fractions of mitochondria for different cell types are shown in Table 3. Chondrocytes in the hypertrophic zone have the lowest volume fraction of mitochondria despite having the highest weight fraction. The volume fraction of mitochondria in hepatocytes is 0.18, consistent with the findings of Loud.
3. Isolated chondrocyte mitochondria consume oxygen in the presence of succinate as a substrate, but the consumption rate is not stimulated by the addition of ADP. This is in contrast to most intact mitochondria, where ADP addition increases the consumption rate severalfold, indicating a transition from the resting state to the active state. This defines the respiratory control ratio (RCR). Chondrocyte mitochondria show strong coupling of substrate oxidation to energy conservation, as evidenced by the inhibitory effect of oligomycin and the stimulatory effect of the uncoupler 1799 on the oxygen consumption rate. Calcium ions stimulate oxygen uptake in chondrocyte mitochondria, and this effect is inhibited by ruthenium red. Oxygen consumption is not observed in isolated chondrocytes until digitonin is added to permeabilize the cells' plasma membrane.
4. Rates of oxygen consumption by isolated mitochondria and digitonin-treated cells are shown in Tables 4 and 5 for growth plate and hyaline cartilage, as well as for liver as a comparison. Calcium ions stimulate chondrocyte mitochondria to the maximal oxygen consumption rate.
5. The method described in this report allows for the isolation of chondrocytes that retain their cellular architecture and size characteristics. Mitochondria can be isolated from these cells, despite the presence of non-mitochondrial proteins in the preparations. The respiratory responses of chondrocyte mitochondria can be compared with those in situ using digitonin-treated chondrocytes.

**Conclusion:** The article provides information on the weight and volume fractions of mitochondria in different cell types, as well as the oxygen consumption rates of isolated chondrocyte mitochondria. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. Additional information is needed on the specific steps and reagents used for isolation.

summary:
Summary 4:
A study by Murphy et al (29) and Loud (24) explores the respiratory responses of isolated hepatocytes and chondrocytes. The weight fraction of hepatocytes is 0.23, consistent with previous findings. Chondrocytes in the hypertrophic zone have the lowest volume fraction of mitochondria, despite having the highest weight fraction. The value of 0.18 obtained for the mitochondrial volume fraction of hepatocytes is in agreement with that of Loud (24). Isolated chondrocyte mitochondria consume oxygen in the presence of succinate as an oxidizable substrate. The consumption rate is not stimulated by ADP but is strongly coupled to energy conservation. Oligomycin inhibits this process, while the uncoupler 1799 stimulates it. Ca2+ stimulates oxygen uptake, which is characteristic of energy-linked uptake by the mitochondria. This effect is inhibited by ruthenium red, a specific inhibitor for this uptake in mitochondria from other tissues. Oxygen consumption is observed in isolated chondrocytes only after permeabilization with digitonin. The method for isolating chondrocytes provides intact cells with retained cellular architecture. Isolated mitochondria from chondrocytes, despite containing nonmitochondrial protein, are fully functional. The respiratory responses of chondrocyte mitochondria can be compared with in situ mitochondria using digitonin-treated chondrocytes.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The cellular mitochondrial volume fraction was determined from 47-50 randomly selected in vivo growth plate and hyaline chondrocytes using the Zeiss MOP-3 system.
2. The mean hepatocyte cellular volume was obtained from the literature.
3. All cells were assumed to have a spherical geometry except for the proliferative chondrocytes, which were calculated as having elliptical dimensions.
4. Mitochondrial volume was taken to be 3.76 pL/mg mitochondrial protein.
5. The isolated mitochondria provide a calibration for the whole cell made permeable by digitonin, which makes it possible to use the latter to study mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake and release.
6. The preparation of the whole cells is more readily available than the preparation of isolated mitochondria, which can be prepared in relatively pure form only in low yield.
7. Cells made permeable by digitonin provide a more physiological environment for mitochondria.
8. The 0, uptake responses of mitochondria, both isolated mitochondria and in digitonin-treated cells, show that chondrocyte mitochondria can couple energy conservation to electron transport to 0, nearly as well as liver mitochondria.
9. The ratio of the uncoupler-stimulated rate to the oligomycin-inhibited rate was 3.6 for isolated growth plate mitochondria and 8.6 for isolated hyaline cartilage mitochondria, compared with 6.7 for isolated liver mitochondria.
10. The RCRs observed in the digitonin-treated cells were 2.6 for growth plate chondrocytes, 6.3 for hyaline cartilage chondrocytes, and 4.1 for hepatocytes.
11. The lack of stimulation of the 0, consumption rate by added ADP in the presence of phosphate, observed with both isolated and in situ chondrocyte mitochondria, appears to be due to a nonspecific cytosolic ATPase activity or ADP utilization.
12. ADP does not further increase the State 4 rate with succinate, but oligomycin inhibits it.
13. The lower control ratios for growth plate mitochondria are most reasonably attributed to their higher endogenous Ca2+ content.
14. Isolated crude mitochondria contain considerable microsomal and lysosomal activity.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides several steps for mitochondrial isolation, including the determination of cellular mitochondrial volume fraction, the use of isolated mitochondria as calibration for whole cells made permeable by digitonin, and the observation of 0, uptake responses and respiratory control ratios in different cell types. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, and some steps, such as the specific method for isolating mitochondria, are missing.

The volume fraction of mitochondria in chondro- 
cytes obtained in this study, using the cytochrome 
aa3 method, ranged from 0.08 for hyaline cartilage 
to 0.09, 0.11, and 0.02 for the reserve, proliferative, 
and hypertrophic zones of the growth plate, re- 
spectively (Table 3) Point-counting analysis of the 
rat epiphyseai plate in vivo had yielded the figure 
of 0.02-0.04 for these chondrocytes (8) It should 
be emphasized that the wide range of mitochondrial 
volume fraction values for the growth plate zones 
arises from the wide range in cell size The chon- 
drocytes from the hypertrophic zone, which have 
0.02 volume fraction mitochondria, are much larger 
in volume than chondrocytes from the other two 
zones and hyaline cartilage, although this is not re- 
flected in increased cellular protein This disparity 
suggests that the more useful index of mitochon- 
drial content may be weight fraction, which relates 
mitochondrial protein to cellular protein The latter 
should give a more realistic index of the enzymatic 
activities in the cell as compared with cellular 
volume Additionally, the difference between the 
weight and volume fraction must be due to the pres- 
ence in the hypertrophic chondrocyte of a nonpro- 
teinaceous substance, probably water 
Comparison of the respiratory activity and cyto- 
chrome aa3 content of chondrocyte mitochondria 
shows the two to be similar, based on corrected 
mitochondrial protein content Chondrocyte mito- 
chondria have slightly more cytochrome aa3 than 
liver mitochondria (29) at 0.31 and 0.25 nmol/mg 
protein, respectively The State 3 0, uptake with 
ADP is faster in liver mitochondria as compared 
with chondrocyte mitochondria; whereas the Ca2+ 
uptake rate is the same or slower (Tables 4 and 5) 
These observations imply that chondrocyte mito- 
chondria closely resemble mitochondria from the 
other cells in their enzymatic capabilities The low 
rate of 0, uptake by chondrocytes may be ascribed 
solely to the low content of mitochondria in the 
cells This low content is also consistent with the 
observation that glycolysis, rather than oxidative 
phosphorylation, provides most of the chondrocyte 
ATP (7,15,22,33) It is also consistent with the con- 
cept that chondrocyte mitochondria are more in- 
volved in calcium transport than in oxidative 
phosphorylation 
J Orthop Res, Vol 2, No, 3, 1984 GROWTH PLATE MITOCHONDRIA 245 
It is doubtful that the predigestion of the cartilage 
using trypsin and hyaluronidase accounts for the 
observed differences between the chondrocytes and 
hepatocytes since the isolated chondrocytes consist- 
ently excluded trypan blue; exhibited no net Ca2 + 
flux across the cell membrane; exhibited no change 
in respiration on addition of exogenous substrate 
(ADP); and had no leakage of cytosolic LDH 
The inclusion of hepatocytes and hepatocyte mito- 
chondria served as a control for a nonmineralized 
tissue, which is readily available, inexpensive, 
and well characterized in the literature (3,9,16, 
17,24,28,29,41,46) Thus, the difference between 
hepatocyte and chondrocyte metabolism reported 
here seems unlikely to be due to differences in cel- 
lular isolation techniques 
CONCLUSIONS 
1 Mammalian chondrocyte mitochondria dem- 
onstrate calcium transport and ADP phosphoryla- 
tion bioenergetics characteristic of other viable 
mammalian cell types 
2 The chondrocyte mitochondria demonstrate a 
blunted respiratory response to ADP as compared 
with its calcium response This may reflect spe- 
cialization of the growth plate mitochondria for cal- 
cium transport and support its role in growth plate 
calcification 
3 In the hypertrophic chondrocyte the mitochon- 
drial weight (protein) fraction is greater than that in 
the proliferative and reserve zone chondrocyte 
Conversely, the mitochondrial volume fraction of 
the hypertrophic chondrocyte is smaller

The volume fraction of mitochondria varies across different zones of the growth plate, with hypertrophic chondrocytes having a higher weight fraction. Chondrocyte mitochondria closely resemble mitochondria from other cells in terms of enzymatic capabilities. The low rate of oxygen uptake by chondrocytes may be attributed to their low mitochondrial content. Chondrocyte mitochondria are involved in calcium transport rather than oxidative phosphorylation. The observed differences between chondrocytes and hepatocytes are unlikely due to differences in cellular isolation techniques.
